next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 6

The Council chamber had seventeen chairs, and Sable Orm knew the occupant of each by the sound they made sitting down. Councilor Breve's knees cracked. Councilor Hain breathed through his mouth when he thought no one was listening. Lady Aldraste settled into her chair without a sound. Around this table, that counted as rank.

Sable had spent twenty-three years in this room. She had organized its agendas, drafted its minutes, revised its protocols, and, on four occasions she did not discuss, corrected the arithmetic of Council members who outranked her. To her, the chamber was a ledger with furniture: every column aligned, every entry predictable.

What she did not know was why she had been summoned to stand before it rather than sit behind the recording desk.

"The Aldraste situation," Councilor Breve began.

Sable waited. Across two decades of administrative service, she had learned that the first sentence of a Council address was rarely the one that mattered.

"Young Lord Aldraste has been outside the walls for approximately five days." Breve checked his notes. The gesture itself was an indictment. "He has spent an estimated twelve levels. In five days. The perimeter enforcement detail authorized under standing recovery protocol made contact yesterday. It did not recover him."

Around the table, hands stopped over paper. Twelve levels. In the Vault, the number was obscene. Sable's nearest colleague had spent four across an entire career, two on a Perception ability during an emergency audit and two on a Light charm during a power failure that threatened the r...
open_hooks_to_carry: Kem learning physical competence without abilities — cooking, fire, walking distance
Vel's origin story: the drowning girl, the Hoarded walking past, her first level gone
Kem's truth about his father — not the politics, the reality
A meal that costs nothing and means something
prev_tail_excerpt:
...se the Council wanted him back in presentable condition, or because forty-one levels gone in one morning was survivable math. It came from someplace lower and less articulate. His blood was up. His body hurt everywhere and felt awake in every hurt place. He had broken things. He had been broken against things. The world had hit him back and discovered he was there.

He should have hated that. A decent version of himself probably would have.

Instead he kept seeing the moment before the first pulse left him, the impossible rush after, the way the forest had changed shape around his mistakes. Shame moved beside the memory, heavy as wet wool. So did a mean, bright delight that refused to apologize.

Vel glanced back once, saw his face, and looked away again as if she had confirmed a fear.

The creek bent south. Mud took the place of stone for a stretch, sucking at his boots. Branches knitted overhead and cut the light down to green shade. Behind them, somewhere above the banks, lay the wrecked basin, the broken trail, the six agents, the tracker, the papers, the proof. Ahead of them lay more trees, less certainty, and whatever kind of education Vel intended to extract from him before the next team arrived.

Kem adjusted the pack higher against his shoulders. The binding around his ribs pulled tight. The Ledger rubbed at him from the inside with every step. It sat at 245.

Vel's three levels had dropped people clean. His forty-one levels had torn a hillside loose. The comparison was absurd, humiliating, and completely correct.

The grin came back anyway.

He kept walking south.

setups_to_plant.description: Vel's story of her first spend at seventeen — saving a drowning girl the Hoarded ignored — establishes her pattern of spending for others, which her later use of Kem will complicate

state_deltas_required: The intimacy of shared vulnerability; Vel's resistance to connection
Real trust begins; both characters become people to each other rather than opposing symbols
